cases.title: Inferior STEMI with a Twist
cases.presentation: A 58-year-old man presents to the ED with crushing substernal chest pain radiating to the right arm, diaphoresis, and nausea. 12-lead ECG shows 3 mm ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. Blood pressure is 82/50 mmHg, heart rate is 52 bpm. The triage nurse reaches for sublingual nitroglycerin.
cases.question: What is the most important next step before administering nitroglycerin?
options: A: Administer nitroglycerin — it is always indicated for chest pain.
B: Obtain right-sided ECG lead V4R to evaluate for right ventricular infarction before giving nitroglycerin.
C: Administer morphine for pain instead.
D: Send the patient directly to the cath lab without any medications.
cases.explanation: Inferior STEMI with hypotension is the classic presentation of right ventricular infarction. Right-sided lead V4R must be obtained — ST elevation ≥1 mm confirms RV infarction, which is an absolute contraindication to nitroglycerin because reduced preload can cause catastrophic hypotension in RV-dependent patients.
cases.clinicalPearl: Always suspect RV infarction in inferior STEMI with hypotension. Get V4R before reaching for nitroglycerin — it could save the patient's life.

cases.title: The Weekend Transfer
cases.presentation: EMS is transporting a 67-year-old woman from a rural clinic with acute-onset chest pain. Symptom onset was 3 hours ago. The 12-lead ECG transmitted en route shows anterior STEMI. The nearest PCI-capable center is 90 minutes away by ground transport. The community hospital where the patient is currently being treated has a CT scanner but no cath lab capability.
cases.question: What is the most appropriate management given the anticipated PPCI delay?
options: A: Wait for transfer to PCI center — fibrinolysis is never appropriate.
B: Administer fibrinolysis (onset <12 hours, anticipated PPCI delay >120 min), then transfer to PCI center for early angiography within 3–24 hours.
C: Administer only aspirin and nitroglycerin — no fibrinolysis.
D: Perform coronary angiography at the community hospital.
cases.explanation: With symptom onset <12 hours and an anticipated PPCI delay exceeding 120 minutes (90 min transport + cath lab activation), fibrinolysis is indicated. After successful fibrinolysis (≥50% ST resolution at 60–90 min), the patient should be transferred for routine early angiography within 3–24 hours. Failed fibrinolysis warrants rescue PCI.
cases.clinicalPearl: Fibrinolysis = '12 and 120' — onset within 12 hours AND anticipated PPCI delay >120 minutes. Always follow fibrinolysis with transfer to a PCI center.

cases.title: The Normoxic Patient
cases.presentation: A 72-year-old man with a history of diabetes and hypertension arrives by EMS with acute chest pain. ECG shows lateral ST depression and T-wave inversions. SpO₂ is 98% on room air. The paramedic wants to place the patient on high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask. Troponin is pending.
cases.question: What is the correct management regarding supplemental oxygen?
options: A: High-flow oxygen is always beneficial in chest pain.
B: No supplemental oxygen — SpO₂ is 98%; routine oxygen in normoxic ACS patients provides no benefit and may cause coronary vasoconstriction.
C: Oxygen is mandatory for all cardiac patients regardless of SpO₂.
D: Only give oxygen if the patient requests it.
cases.explanation: The 2025 guidelines recommend supplemental oxygen ONLY for SpO₂ <90% or respiratory distress. The DETO2X-AMI trial demonstrated that routine supplemental oxygen in normoxic ACS patients provides no benefit and may increase coronary vasoconstriction. This patient's SpO₂ is 98% — oxygen is not indicated.
cases.clinicalPearl: O₂ only <90% — routine oxygen in normoxic ACS patients is not supported by evidence and may worsen coronary perfusion.
